title: NASA Task Load Index (NASA-TLX)
description: 6-subscale multidimensional rating procedure for assessing perceived workload. Widely used in VR studies as a proxy for cognitive load and mental effort.
citation: Hart, S.G., & Staveland, L.E. (1988). Development of NASA-TLX (Task Load Index): Results of empirical and theoretical research. Advances in Psychology, 52, 139-183.

MD.name: Mental Demand
MD.question: How much mental and perceptual activity was required (e.g., thinking, deciding, calculating, remembering, looking, searching, etc.)? Was the task easy or demanding, simple or complex, exacting or forgiving?

PD.name: Physical Demand
PD.question: How much physical activity was required (e.g., pushing, pulling, turning, controlling, activating, etc.)? Was the task easy or demanding, slow or brisk, slack or strenuous, restful or laborious?

TD.name: Temporal Demand
TD.question: How much time pressure did you feel due to the rate or pace at which the tasks or task elements occurred? Was the pace slow and leisurely or rapid and frantic?

OP.name: Performance
OP.question: How successful do you think you were in accomplishing the goals of the task set by the experimenter (or yourself)? How satisfied were you with your performance in accomplishing these goals?
OP.note: This subscale is REVERSED — lower raw score indicates better performance; invert when computing workload composite.

EF.name: Effort
EF.question: How hard did you have to work (mentally and physically) to accomplish your level of performance?

FR.name: Frustration
FR.question: How insecure, discouraged, irritated, stressed, and annoyed were you?

raw_TLX.description: Unweighted mean of all 6 subscales (most commonly used in VR/HCI research)
raw_TLX.formula: TLX_raw = mean(MD, PD, TD, 100-OP, EF, FR)
raw_TLX.note: OP must be reversed before averaging

weighted_TLX.description: Pairwise comparison weighting — participants rank subscale importance via 15 pairwise comparisons; each subscale's weight = number of times chosen / 15
weighted_TLX.note: More valid for between-subject comparisons; adds ~5 minutes to administration

administration.validated_in: Aviation, HCI, VR, surgical simulation, driving simulation

subscale_relevance.MD: Sensitive to VR task complexity and navigation demands
subscale_relevance.PD: Sensitive to controller interaction and locomotion method
subscale_relevance.TD: Sensitive to task pacing in VR scenarios
subscale_relevance.FR: Correlates with cybersickness and poor usability

vr_specific_notes.correlation_with_physiological: MD and EF subscales correlate most strongly with EEG theta power and fNIRS HbO increases in prefrontal cortex
